original_abstract: Large language models (LLMs) pretrained on vast source code have achieved prominent progress in code intelligence. However, existing code LLMs have two main limitations. First, they often adopt a specific architecture (encoder-only or decoder-only) or rely on a unified encoder-decoder network for different downstream tasks, lacking the flexibility to operate in the optimal architecture for a specific task. Secondly, they often employ a limited set of pretraining objectives which might not be relevant to some tasks and hence result in substantial performance degrade. To address these limitations, we propose “CodeT5+”, a family of encoder-decoder LLMs for code in which component modules can be flexibly combined to suit a wide range of code tasks. Such flexibility is enabled by our proposed mixture of pretraining objectives, which cover span denoising, contrastive learning, text-code matching, and causal LM pretraining tasks, on both unimodal and bimodal multilingual code corpora. Furthermore, we propose to initialize CodeT5+ with frozen off-the-shelf LLMs without training from scratch to efficiently scale up our models, and explore instruction-tuning to align with natural language instructions. We extensively evaluate CodeT5+ on over 20 code-related benchmarks in different settings, including zero-shot, finetuning, and instruction-tuning. We observe state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance on various code-related tasks, and our instruction-tuned CodeT5+ 16B achieves new SoTA results of 35.0% pass@1 and 54.5% pass@10 on the HumanEval code generation task against other open code LLMs, even surpassing the OpenAI code-cushman-001 model.
humanized_abstract: Prior research on large language models (LLMs) that are pretrained on a large amount of source code has yielded significant achievements in code intelligence. However, the current code LLMs have two main shortcomings. On one hand, many state-of-the-art code LLMs use a fixed architecture for code intelligence applications regardless of whether the applications are based on an encoder only, a decoder only, or both an encoder and a decoder, as demonstrated in Table 1, which results in unsatisfactory performance for particular code intelligence applications. This shortcoming can be referred to some extent attributed to the inflexibility of a unified encoder-decoder architecture. On the other hand, almost all state-of-the-art code LLMs share the same set of pretraining objectives and hence inevitably suffer from performance drops due to limited and suboptimal fit to specific code intelligence applications, as illustrated in Table 2. To overcome the above-mentioned challenges, we propose CodeT5+, a new generation of encoder-decoder large language models designed specifically for code. The CodeT5+ models consist of a series of flexible modules (encoder and/or decoder) that can be assembled differently for diverse code intelligence applications, where a variety of pretraining objectives tailored to specific code intelligence applications are used. To train CodeT5+, a mix of the pretraining objectives including span denoising, contrastive learning, text-code matching and causal language model are fine-tuned on a large-scale unimodal and bimodal multilingual code corpus. Additionally, to facilitate the scaling up of CodeT5+, we recommend that the new model is initialized by learning from pre-trained, off-the-shelf LLMs in a frozen way instead of from scratch as demonstrated in Table 3, and further adopt the instruction-tuning method by letting the CodeT5+ model learn how to comply with the natural language instructions given to human experts in coding. Extensive experiments on over 20 code-related benchmarks under three settings, namely, the zero-shot setting, finetuning setting, and the instruction-tuning setting, show that CodeT5+ significantly outperforms all state-of-the-art code LLMs. Notably, in terms of code generation benchmarks, the instruction-tuned CodeT5+ 16B yields a significantly better performance than OpenAI code-cushman-001 model with new state-of-the-art (SoTA) results of 35.0% pass@1 and 54.5% pass@10 achieved on the challenging benchmark of HumanEval.

On one hand, many state-of-the-art code LLMs use a fixed architecture for code intelligence applications regardless of whether the applications are based on an encoder only, a decoder only, or both an encoder and a decoder, as demonstrated in Table 1, which results in unsatisfactory performance for particular code intelligence applications. On the other hand, almost all state-of-the-art code LLMs share the same set of pretraining objectives and hence inevitably suffer from performance drops due to limited and suboptimal fit to specific code intelligence applications, as illustrated in Table 2.
